title: City of Chaos - The Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qkMCHJ28vsI

Daniel (@dungeondive) delivers his verdict on City of Chaos, the rare late-1990s game (~2,000 made) he paid around $150 for on eBay. His honest conclusion: it is not a good *game*, but it is a remarkable *work of interactive fiction* — one he's glad to own and will never sell, since his adventure-game collection wouldn't feel complete without it.

What he loves: the way the story unfolds, the richly realised world, and prose that — while occasionally clunky and no Cisko or Ligotti — sits at the very top of board-game writing. Above all he praises the clue-card system, which makes you *think* and connect discoveries rather than handing you a guaranteed breadcrumb trail; a full playthrough leaves most of the vast Tome of Chaos, action deck, clues and items unseen, which he prizes.

What he doesn't: nearly everything mechanical. Poor, endlessly-patched rules; unfun dice-roll movement across bland, near-identical tiles that exist only to force encounters; a punishing three-deaths-and-out rule; and slow, deadly combat. He demos a streamlined location-to-location variant — move orthogonally to the next unexplored location, roll for an encounter en route — hoping to someday experience one complete narrative arc.

----

Would you pay collector's prices for a game you admit isn't *good* but is a one-of-a-kind experience — where's the line for you between collection and play?
